assembled.architecture: Excalidraw is an ESM-only TypeScript monorepo of layered npm packages (all v0.18.0 except @excalidraw/utils v0.1.2) feeding one publishable React component library. The dependency layering is strict and bottom-up: @excalidraw/math (pure, dependency-free 2D geometry) <- @excalidraw/common (shared constants/utils/url-sanitizer, depends on math) <- @excalidraw/element (the canonical ExcalidrawElement data model, Scene store, Store/undo-redo substrate, binding, fractional-index ordering; runtime-deps only common + math) <- @excalidraw/utils (thin export-to-image/clipboard + geometry façade) and @excalidraw/excalidraw (the App god-component + public API barrel). Every package exposes a single barrel index that re-exports its modules wholesale, and the public API of @excalidraw/excalidraw deliberately AGGREGATES symbols re-exported from the sibling packages. A pervasive but TYPE-ONLY cycle exists: element imports ~35 type-only symbols from its own consumer @excalidraw/excalidraw (AppState/App/etc.), erased at compile time, so it is a layering cycle with zero runtime/bundle edge. The data model is JSON-serializable with no peer-local state so it can be shared between collaboration peers; collaboration/undo-redo is built on per-element version (sequential int) + versionNonce (random tie-breaker) and a Store that emits CaptureUpdateAction-tagged increments.

components.name: @excalidraw/math (packages/math)
components.role: Dependency-free 2D geometry primitives: branded point/vector/line/segment/curve/ellipse/polygon/triangle/rectangle/range/angle types plus pure (never-mutating) construction, intersection, distance, rotation and Bezier arc-length functions.

components.name: @excalidraw/common
components.role: Foundational shared-utilities barrel: app-wide constants/enums (constants.ts), environment detection, AppEventBus/Emitter/Queue, the seeded global PRNG, url sanitization, colors, keys, and feature flags; depends on @excalidraw/math at runtime (not a pure leaf).

components.name: @excalidraw/element
components.role: The element-model core: the canonical ExcalidrawElement discriminated union, mutateElement/newElementWith versioning, the in-memory Scene store, the Store/undo-redo+collaboration delta substrate, arrow/text binding, bounds/collision, duplication, and fractional-index ordering.

components.name: fractional-indexing
components.role: Two-layer z-order system: a vendored (CC0) dependency-free base-62 fractional-indexing algorithm (packages/fractional-indexing) plus the Excalidraw-domain layer (element/src/fractionalIndex.ts) that keeps each element's string index in sync with array order, repairing invalid indices during reconcile/restore.

components.name: @excalidraw/utils
components.role: Browser-only public package: a thin export-to-image/clipboard orchestration layer over @excalidraw/excalidraw plus a roughjs/element-independent pure-geometry collision toolkit built on @excalidraw/math.

components.name: @excalidraw/excalidraw
components.role: The publishable React editor component library: owns the public API barrel (index.tsx) and the ~13k-line App god-component that constructs and wires Scene, Store, History, Renderer, ActionManager, Fonts, an isolated Jotai store, and the import/export/collaboration data layer.

critical_to_know.claim: Undo/redo capture is governed by a CaptureUpdateAction tri-state on EVERY scene mutation: IMMEDIATELY (local edits, undoable), NEVER (remote/collab updates and scene init, never on the undo stack), EVENTUALLY (deferred, the documented default). Mis-tagging a remote update as IMMEDIATELY corrupts the undo stack — the key cross-cutting state-change invariant.

Collaboration is deterministically version-based: keep local when actively editing OR local.version > remote.version OR (equal versions) local.versionNonce <= remote.versionNonce — the equal-version tie is broken by the LOWEST versionNonce so all peers converge identically. version is a sequential int, versionNonce a per-change random int.

VALUE-SEMANTICS SPLIT: mutateElement() mutates in place, returns the SAME object, and does NOT trigger a re-render — callers needing a re-render must use scene.mutateElement / ExcalidrawImperativeAPI.mutateElement. newElementWith() is the immutable counterpart (returns a new spread, short-circuits to the original when nothing changed unless force=true).

VERSIONING INVARIANT: a real change via mutateElement bumps element.version, regenerates versionNonce via randomInteger(), and stamps updated — but ONLY when didChange is true; an update equal to the current value is skipped (no version bump). Every element must have version > 0 so getSceneVersion detects new elements (restore coerces version || 1).

ShapeCache is a static WeakMap keyed by element object identity; because mutateElement returns the same reference, mutations must explicitly invalidate it — mutateElement calls ShapeCache.delete(element) whenever height, width, fileId, or points change, and generateElementShape always bypasses+does-not-store the cache when isExporting is true.

DUAL ORDERING INVARIANT: element ordering is held redundantly in BOTH array order AND each element's fractional `index` string, which MUST be kept in sync; the array is a CACHE of the order defined by fractional indices. validateFractionalIndices throws InvalidFractionalIndexError on violation; syncInvalidIndices repairs without altering already-valid indices.

App constructs core subsystems in a FIXED, load-bearing order in its constructor: actionManager -> scene(new Scene()) -> renderer(new Renderer(scene)) -> store(new Store(this)) -> history(new History(store)) -> fonts(new Fonts(scene)) — History depends on Store, Renderer/Fonts depend on Scene. Teardown(destroy) runs the matching reverse-ordered release sequence.

State flows ONE direction Store -> History: store.onDurableIncrementEmitter drives history.record; History is purely a downstream listener, never the driver. History.record refuses re-entrant capture (early-returns on empty delta or instanceof HistoryDelta) and only clears the redo stack when the delta has non-empty ELEMENT changes (a bare appState change must not wipe redo).

Undo/redo is treated as a NEW user action, not literal time-travel: applying a history delta explicitly EXCLUDES version/versionNonce from restoration (excludedProperties Set), so each undo/redo yields a fresh version for collaboration/sync.

random.ts holds a MODULE-LEVEL mutable singleton PRNG seeded once from Date.now() at import; randomInteger() draws from it and only reseed(seed) makes it deterministic — a hidden cross-call coupling. randomId() returns sequential ids ('id0','id1',...) in test env and nanoid() otherwise, so id values silently differ test-vs-prod.

Bundler-injected import.meta.env.MODE is a load-bearing global: isTestEnv/isDevEnv/isProdEnv gate behavior across common (random ids, timestamps, the AppEventBus once-guard). AppEventBus's 'once'-cardinality throw-on-double-emit is enforced ONLY when !isProdEnv() — that safety check is silently DISABLED in production.

PRECISION literal trap: 'export const PRECISION = 10e-5' evaluates to 1e-4 (0.0001), NOT 1e-5. It is defined TWICE with the same value — the package-wide tolerance is utils.ts's PRECISION (imported by angle/point/segment/polygon/ellipse); constants.ts's identically-named PRECISION sits by the Legendre-Gauss tables and is NOT the tolerance source.

All @excalidraw/math geometric types are NOMINAL/branded (phantom _brand intersected with the structural shape); a plain number[] cannot be passed where a branded type is expected — values must be built via factory functions (pointFrom, vector, lineSegment). Line, LineSegment and Rectangle are the SAME 2-tuple-of-points at runtime, distinguished only by brand. Every math op returns a new value and never mutates inputs.

FONT_FAMILY maps font name to a STABLE integer id and explicitly reserves id 4 as permanently unused (historically Assistant/custom font) — these ids are a persisted/serialization contract, so the gap at 4 is intentional and must not be reused.

@excalidraw/common's barrel re-exports Debug from '../debug' — a sibling file OUTSIDE the src/ tree, so the public surface deliberately reaches one directory up; scanning only src/ misses that Debug is public API. Many common modules also assume a BROWSER runtime (editorInterface reads navigator at module load; utils/colors touch window/document/localStorage), so importing common in a non-DOM context can throw at import time unless guarded.

url.ts is the single security chokepoint for untrusted links: normalizeLink runs @braintree/sanitize-url after escaping double-quotes, and toValidURL returns 'about:blank' for anything that fails URL parsing — sanitize hrefs/iframe src here before render.

BINDING IS BIDIRECTIONAL: a bindable element lists dependents in boundElements ({id,type:'arrow'|'text'}) while arrows hold startBinding/endBinding and text holds containerId; fixBindingsAfterDeletion walks BOTH directions on every deleted element to keep the two-way refs consistent. Duplication assigns a fresh randomId() and runs fixDuplicatedBindingsAfterDuplication so bindings point at copies (original id preserved under an ORIG_ID symbol).

appState.ts's APP_STATE_STORAGE_CONF is the single source of truth for which AppState fields persist to {browser, export, server}; serializeAsJSON strips per target from it. Adding a new AppState field without an entry here SILENTLY fails to persist (the loop skips keys with no propConfig), and getDefaultAppState must be kept in lock-step by string key with no enforced shared key list.

The editor uses an ISOLATED Jotai store via jotai-scope createIsolation (editorJotaiStore), NOT the global Jotai store, so multiple Excalidraw instances and the host app's atoms do not collide; index.tsx wraps App in EditorJotaiProvider. The host reaches the running editor via React-context-bridged imperative API (ExcalidrawAPIContext + useExcalidrawAPI), the supported boundary for external control.

NON-OBVIOUS TYPE-ONLY CYCLE: ~35 @excalidraw/element source files import types from their own consumer @excalidraw/excalidraw (AppState/App/PointerDownState/etc.) — but EVERY such import is `import type` (erased at compile time) with ZERO runtime imports, and package.json declares no app dependency. It is a compile-time layering cycle, invisible to the runtime/bundle graph.

@excalidraw/utils ALWAYS normalizes inbound elements through restoreElements (deleteInvisibleElements:true) + restoreAppState before any export — exporters assume restored, not raw, input. PNG-with-embedded-scene deliberately serializes the ORIGINAL uncloned opts.elements (not the rendered restoredElements) to keep ids stable across the Scene hack — a subtle identity invariant a refactor could break. The package is browser-only (calls document.createElement directly, no env guard).

common's colors.ts carries a documented 'FIXME can't put to utils.ts rn because of circular dependency' that forced a local duplicate of `pick`: constants.ts imports COLOR_PALETTE from colors.ts while colors helpers want utils — a real, acknowledged constants<->colors<->utils cycle. (IF-8 candidate, GATED.)

Scene.replaceAllElements is the single funnel for setting scene contents, establishing the order: syncInvalidIndices (validate indices) -> rebuild elementsMap -> derive nonDeleted element/frame collections -> triggerUpdate() notify. fractional indices are repaired on every restore (restore.ts) and are the cross-client ordering authority during reconcile.

extractions.role: A foundational shared-utilities package that the rest of the Excalidraw monorepo imports via a single barrel.

facts.claim: This is the @excalidraw/common package (v0.18.0); src/index.ts is a barrel that re-exports every public module, so consumers import from the package root, not deep paths.

The barrel's LAST line re-exports `Debug` from `../debug`, i.e. from packages/common/debug.ts which sits OUTSIDE the src/ tree (a sibling file, not a src module) — so the package's public surface deliberately reaches one directory up; a new engineer scanning only src/ would miss that Debug is part of the public API.

constants.ts is the single source of truth for app-wide tunables and enums (thresholds, zoom limits MIN_ZOOM/MAX_ZOOM, FONT_FAMILY id map, MIME_TYPES, THEME, ROUNDNESS, TOOL_TYPE, the EVENT enum, etc.); it is the most-imported module and the de-facto configuration surface for the whole editor.

FONT_FAMILY maps font name to a STABLE integer id and explicitly reserves id 4 as permanently unused ('leave 4 unused as it was historically used for Assistant ... or custom font') — these ids are a persisted/serialization contract, so the gap at 4 is intentional and must not be reused.

random.ts holds a MODULE-LEVEL MUTABLE singleton `random` seeded once from Date.now() at import time; randomInteger() draws from it and reseed(seed) replaces it. This shared global PRNG is a hidden cross-call coupling — determinism in tests depends entirely on calling reseed(), not on any per-call argument.

randomId() returns deterministic sequential ids ('id0','id1',...) in the test env (isTestEnv()) and nanoid() otherwise — so id values silently differ between test and production; snapshot/equality logic that assumes opaque ids can pass in tests yet behave differently in prod.

Environment is detected via import.meta.env.MODE compared against the ENV constants (isTestEnv/isDevEnv/isProdEnv); these gate behavior across the package (random ids, getUpdatedTimestamp, the AppEventBus once-guard), making the bundler-injected MODE a load-bearing global dependency.

AppEventBus enforces 'once' cardinality (throw on a second emit of a once-event) ONLY when !isProdEnv() — the duplicate-emit invariant is a DEV-ONLY assertion that is silently disabled in production, so a double-emit bug can pass prod unnoticed.

AppEventBus replay='last' delivers the cached payload to a late subscriber asynchronously via queueMicrotask (not synchronously), so a subscriber added after emit() will NOT have run by the time on() returns — an ordering subtlety for code expecting immediate replay.

Queue serializes async jobs strictly: a `running` boolean guard makes tick() a no-op while a job is in flight, and each job re-triggers tick() in its .finally() — jobs run one-at-a-time in FIFO push order regardless of how fast their promises settle.

VersionedSnapshotStore.set() is a no-op (returns false, no version bump, no notify) when isEqual(prev,next) holds (default Object.is); on a real change it increments version and notifies subscribers, then drains-and-CLEARS the one-shot `waiters` set used by pull() — so pull() waiters fire exactly once per change while subscribe() listeners persist.

VersionedSnapshotStore.pull(sinceVersion) resolves immediately if the current version differs from sinceVersion, otherwise parks the resolver in `waiters` until the next set() — a version-gated long-poll. The sinceVersion default of -1 means an initial pull resolves immediately (version starts at 0).

colors.ts contains an explicit 'FIXME can't put to utils.ts rn because of circular dependency' on a local `pick` helper — a documented workaround acknowledging a constants<->colors<->utils cycle; constants.ts imports COLOR_PALETTE from colors.ts while colors helpers want utils, so the helper was duplicated locally instead.

The dark-mode color cache (DARK_MODE_COLORS_CACHE) is intentionally null when `window` is undefined ('Browser-only cache to avoid memory leaks on server'); applyDarkModeFilter must therefore tolerate a null cache and recompute every call in SSR/server contexts.

Many modules assume a BROWSER runtime: editorInterface.ts reads navigator.platform/userAgent at MODULE LOAD time (isDarwin/isWindows/etc. are evaluated on import), utils.ts touches window/document/localStorage, colors uses window. Importing common in a non-DOM context can throw at import time unless guarded — a hard environment boundary rule.

KEYS.CTRL_OR_CMD is resolved ONCE at import time to 'metaKey' or 'ctrlKey' based on isDarwin (from editorInterface) — keys.ts depends on editorInterface.ts, and the platform branch is frozen at load, not re-evaluated per event.

matchKey() implements a deliberate key-vs-code fallback for non-Latin keyboard layouts: it matches event.key when Latin, else falls back to event.code via KeyCodeMap (currently only Z and Y) — a non-obvious correctness rule documented with a per-layout table; naive event.key comparison would break on Cyrillic/Greek/CJK layouts.

url.ts is the security boundary for untrusted links: normalizeLink runs @braintree/sanitize-url after escaping double-quotes, and toValidURL returns 'about:blank' for anything that fails to parse as a URL — the intended single chokepoint for sanitizing hrefs/iframe src before render.

common imports types and runtime helpers from sibling monorepo packages @excalidraw/math (average, clamp, degreesToRadians, pointFromPair, point types) and type-only from @excalidraw/element/types and @excalidraw/excalidraw/types — so 'common' is NOT a pure leaf: it has a runtime dependency edge to @excalidraw/math.

queue.ts imports promiseTry/resolvablePromise/ResolvablePromise from '.' (the index barrel) rather than directly from './utils' — an intra-package edge that routes through the barrel, a self-import pattern that can aggravate module-init ordering / circular-dependency issues.

FONT_METADATA lookups (getVerticalOffset/getLineHeight) fall back to FONT_FAMILY.Excalifont's metrics for any unknown fontFamily id — there is no error on a missing font; the Excalifont default is the never-throws invariant for font math.

Feature flags are read lazily from localStorage once and memoized in a module-level `featureFlags` var (default {COMPLEX_BINDINGS:false}); getFeatureFlag swallows localStorage errors silently and setFeatureFlag persists JSON — a hidden global mutable cache that does not refresh after first read within a session.

isShallowEqual treats two empty arrays (and empty objects, top- or second-level) as EQUAL via _defaultIsShallowComparatorFallback — a deliberate non-standard equality used by updateStable/updateObject; equality here is NOT plain ===, which is easy to overlook when reasoning about React-style change detection.

Emitter.once() works by appending a self-detaching handler (() => detach()) to the subscriber list, so the auto-unsubscribe fires AFTER the user handlers in the same trigger pass; AppEventBus.on()'s promise form is built on this once() behavior.

candidate_issues.claim: Documented circular dependency among constants <-> colors <-> utils: constants.ts imports COLOR_PALETTE from colors.ts (constants.ts:7), while colors.ts carries a 'FIXME can't put to utils.ts rn because of circular dependency' that forced a local duplicate of `pick`. The cycle is real and acknowledged in-source (a structurally-grounded Shape-A candidate, not a fabrication).

Magic-string localStorage keys are scattered across modules with no shared registry: 'excalidraw-feature-flags' (utils.ts:1286) and 'excalidraw.desktopUIMode' (editorInterface.ts:16), plus the EDITOR_LS_KEYS object in constants.ts:467 — the two former keys live outside EDITOR_LS_KEYS, a cross-module string-coupling/inconsistency where some LS keys are centralized and some are inline literals.

BinaryHeap.bubbleUp has an asymmetry: when checking the right child it updates smallestIdx but NOT smallestScore (binary-heap.ts:43-47), unlike the left-child branch which updates both (lines 35-39). If both children are smaller than the node, the right child is selected without comparing its score against the left child's — a possible heap-property correctness bug. Grounded as a structural anomaly; recorded GATED, not asserted as a defect without an oracle.

AppEventBus's once-cardinality 'can only be emitted once' invariant is enforced only behind !isProdEnv() (appEventBus.ts:104), so the safety check that prevents a double-emit is absent in production — a guarded error path that does not run where it matters most. Recorded GATED.

extractions.role: The element-model core of Excalidraw — owns the canonical ExcalidrawElement data model plus all element-level logic (mutation/versioning, the in-memory Scene store, arrow/text binding, bounds, collision, dragging, resizing, fractional-index ordering, shape generation).

facts.claim: The package is a standalone npm package @excalidraw/element (v0.18.0, MIT, ESM-only) whose declared runtime dependencies are ONLY @excalidraw/common and @excalidraw/math — it does NOT declare a dependency on the consuming @excalidraw/excalidraw app package.

index.ts is the public barrel entry point: it re-exports (export *) every module in the package plus a handful of locally-defined helpers (getSceneVersion, hashElementsVersion, hashString, getVisibleElements, getNonDeletedElements, isNonDeletedElement), so the package surface is effectively every exported symbol of all 49 source files.

NON-OBVIOUS BOUNDARY/CYCLE: the element package pervasively imports types from its OWN consumer, the @excalidraw/excalidraw app (AppState, App, ExcalidrawProps, PointerDownState, ObservedAppState, etc.) across ~35 files — but EVERY such import is `import type` (type-only, erased at compile time) and there are ZERO value/runtime imports from the app. This is a deliberate type-only architectural cycle (element <-> app) that does not exist in the runtime/bundle graph and is invisible to package.json deps.

VALUE-SEMANTICS SPLIT (key invariant): mutateElement() MUTATES the element in place and returns the same object, and its doc WARNING states it does NOT trigger a component re-render — callers needing a re-render must use scene.mutateElement or ExcalidrawImperativeAPI.mutateElement instead.

newElementWith() is the immutable counterpart to mutateElement: it returns a NEW spread object {...element, ...updates} (never mutating the input) and short-circuits to the original element when nothing changed unless force=true is passed.

VERSIONING INVARIANT: any real change via mutateElement bumps element.version (or uses the supplied updates.version), regenerates element.versionNonce via randomInteger(), and stamps element.updated — but ONLY when didChange is true (an update equal to the current value is skipped and returns the element unchanged, no version bump).

version is a sequentially-incremented integer and versionNonce is a per-change random integer; both exist for collaboration reconciliation — version reconciles elements across peers/saves, versionNonce deterministically breaks ties when versions are identical (documented on the base element type).

ShapeCache is a static WeakMap keyed by element object identity (mapping element -> {shape, theme}); because mutateElement returns the same object reference, mutations must explicitly invalidate it. mutateElement calls ShapeCache.delete(element) whenever height, width, fileId, or points change.

ShapeCache.generateElementShape ALWAYS regenerates (bypasses the cache) when renderConfig.isExporting is true, to guarantee the latest shape at export time, and never stores the export-time shape back into the cache.

ELBOW-ARROW SPECIAL CASE (ordering/lifecycle invariant): mutateElement, when the element is an elbow arrow AND (updates is empty | points changed | fixedSegments changed), forces angle:0 and routes the update through updateElbowArrowPoints() before applying — so elbow arrows are re-routed/normalized inside the generic mutate path, not by callers.

DUAL ORDERING INVARIANT: element ordering is held redundantly in BOTH the array order AND each element's fractional `index` (string), and the two MUST be kept in sync; syncInvalidIndices/syncMovedIndices reconcile them and validateFractionalIndices throws InvalidFractionalIndexError when the invariant is violated. The array is documented as a cache of the order defined by fractional indices.

Scene.replaceAllElements is the single funnel for setting scene contents: it runs syncInvalidIndices, rebuilds elementsMap, derives the nonDeleted element/frame collections, and calls triggerUpdate() — establishing the lifecycle order (validate indices -> sync -> rebuild derived maps -> notify callbacks).

Scene is the in-memory element store: it holds elements/elementsMap/nonDeletedElements plus a selectedElements cache and a sceneNonce (a render-cache-invalidation random int, explicitly documented as UNRELATED to element versions), and notifies subscribers via a Set<callback> through triggerUpdate().

Scene.mutateElement is the React-aware wrapper over the free mutateElement function: it only calls triggerUpdate() when the element is actually in the scene's elementsMap, the version changed (prevVersion !== nextVersion), and options.informMutation is true.

Store captures observed scene changes and emits StoreIncrement events; CaptureUpdateAction is a const enum (IMMEDIATELY / NEVER / EVENTUALLY) that controls whether an update is recorded onto the undo/redo stacks — NEVER is used for remote updates and scene init, EVENTUALLY for ephemeral multi-step async ops. This is the undo/redo + collaboration delta substrate.

DUPLICATION INTEGRITY INVARIANT: duplicateElement deep-copies the element (deepCopyElement), assigns a fresh copy.id = randomId(), and the duplication flow later calls fixDuplicatedBindingsAfterDuplication so that arrow/text/frame bindings (boundElements, startBinding/endBinding, containerId) point at the new copies rather than the originals; the original id is preserved under an ORIG_ID symbol key.

BINDING IS BIDIRECTIONAL: a bindable element records its dependents in boundElements ([] of {id,type:'arrow'|'text'}) while arrows hold startBinding/endBinding and text holds containerId; fixBindingsAfterDeletion walks both directions (BoundElement.unbindAffected + BindableElement.unbindAffected) on every deleted element to keep the two-way references consistent after deletion.

updateBoundElements re-routes bound arrows when a bindable element changes, guarded by doesNeedUpdate (skips stale boundElements) and by simultaneouslyUpdatedElementIds (skips arrows already being moved/scaled), then applies point updates via LinearElementEditor.movePoints — i.e. binding propagation is the cross-element coupling triggered by element geometry changes.

ExcalidrawElement is a discriminated union over a string `type` field (selection/rectangle/diamond/ellipse/text/line/arrow/freedraw/image/frame/magicframe/iframe/embeddable), and the entire model is documented as JSON-serializable with NO computed/peer-local state, so the element list is shareable between collaboration peers.

All element types are built on a Readonly base type _ExcalidrawElementBase, so element shape is immutable-typed; mutation is only possible via the explicit Mutable<> casts used in mutateElement/bumpVersion — the type system steers callers away from ad-hoc field assignment.

Geometry/rendering is delegated to roughjs (RoughGenerator) for hand-drawn shape generation; the package depends on @excalidraw/math for all point/vector/radian primitives (headings, e.g., are quantized to one of four unit vectors HEADING_RIGHT/DOWN/LEFT/UP).

Scene.getSelectedElements is memoized via selectedElementsCache keyed by a SelectionHash built from selectedElementIds + the boolean options (includeBoundTextElement/includeElementsInFrames); the cache is bypassed (and not populated) when caller passes custom `elements`, and cleared when operating on stale latest-scene elements.

isInvisiblySmallElement (used by getVisibleElements) treats linear/freedraw elements with <2 points, or 2-point arrows whose endpoints are within INVISIBLY_SMALL_ELEMENT_SIZE (0.1) of each other, as invisible — a load-bearing visibility rule for rendering and the open TODO notes such elements are not yet consistently excluded from store/export/broadcast/persist.

INTERNAL DEPENDENCY EDGES: mutateElement depends on shape.ts (ShapeCache), elbowArrow.ts (updateElbowArrowPoints) and typeChecks.ts (isElbowArrow); Scene.ts depends on the package barrel @excalidraw/element (a self-package import) for predicates/index-sync/selection helpers — so the package imports its own public surface internally.

candidate_issues.claim: Type-only cyclic coupling element <-> @excalidraw/excalidraw: ~35 element source files import types from the consuming app package (its own dependent), and Scene.ts imports the app via a deep relative path (../../excalidraw/types) while every other file uses the @excalidraw/excalidraw/* alias. All imports are `import type` (erased at runtime) and package.json declares no app dependency, so this is a compile-time-only cycle, not a runtime import cycle — structurally real but does NOT produce a runtime/bundle cycle. GATED: report as a layering observation, not a runtime defect.

Scene.ts reaches the app package via the raw relative path "../../excalidraw/types" (line 43) whereas the rest of the package consistently uses the package alias "@excalidraw/excalidraw/types" — an inconsistent import convention for the same target; refactor-rename risk and a divergence from the codebase's own pattern. Type-only so no runtime impact. GATED.

getSceneVersion is exported and explicitly @deprecated ('unsafe, use hashElementsVersion instead') yet remains part of the public barrel surface (export * from ./index helpers), so consumers can still pick it up. Documented dead-ish API retained for back-compat; flag as a tech-debt/legacy-warning, not a defect.

extractions.role: A dependency-free 2D computational-geometry primitives library: branded coordinate/shape types (point, vector, line, segment, curve, ellipse, polygon, triangle, rectangle, range, angle) plus pure functions for construction, intersection, distance, rotation, and Bezier arc-length, consumed by the rest of the Excalidraw editor.

facts.claim: The package is a pure, near-self-contained geometry primitives library: 15 source modules under src/ exposing point/vector/line/segment/curve/ellipse/polygon/triangle/rectangle/range/angle operations; no React, no I/O, no state.

index.ts is the single public barrel entry point; it re-exports every module wholesale via 'export * from', so the public surface is the union of all module exports (there is no curated/hidden surface).

All geometric types are NOMINAL/branded: a structural tuple or object intersected with a phantom '_brand' literal (e.g. GlobalPoint = [x,y] & {_brand:'excalimath__globalpoint'}). A plain number[] cannot be passed where a branded type is expected — values must be constructed/cast through the factory functions (pointFrom, vector, lineSegment, etc.). The brand is compile-time only; at runtime the value is just the underlying array/object.

Points and vectors are represented as 2-element tuples [x,y]/[u,v], NOT objects; the legacy {x,y} object forms (GlobalCoord/LocalCoord) are explicitly marked TODO-to-remove. New code is expected to use the tuple forms — mixing the two is a known migration hazard.

VALUE SEMANTICS / IMMUTABILITY: every operation returns a NEW array/object and never mutates its inputs — e.g. pointTranslate, vectorAdd, vectorSubtract, lineSegmentRotate all build a fresh tuple. Callers can safely share inputs; no operation is in-place.

A single shared tolerance PRECISION governs every 'closeness'/'equality' comparison in the package (pointsEqual, isCloseTo, pointOnLineSegment, pointOnPolygon, ellipseTouchesPoint all default to it). Geometric equality here is approximate, never exact float equality.

PRECISION literal trap: 'export const PRECISION = 10e-5' evaluates to 1e-4 (0.0001), NOT 1e-5 — '10e-5' is 10 x 10^-5. A reader expecting 1e-5 would be off by a factor of 10 on the default tolerance used across the whole package.

PRECISION is defined TWICE under two different names with the SAME value: 'PRECISION = 10e-5' in constants.ts AND 'PRECISION = 10e-5' in utils.ts. utils.ts's PRECISION is the one imported by angle/point/segment/polygon/ellipse; constants.ts's PRECISION sits next to the Legendre-Gauss tables and is not the package-wide tolerance source.

Angle units are encoded in the type system (Radians vs Degrees brands) and the CONVENTION is radians-internal: rotation/normalization/right-angle logic all operate on Radians (pointRotateRads, normalizeRadians, isRightAngleRads); the *Degs variants are thin adapters that convert via degreesToRadians first. Callers must pass radians to the core paths.

normalizeRadians always returns an angle in [0, 2*PI): for negative input it adds 2*PI after the modulo, guaranteeing a non-negative result. radiansBetweenAngles relies on this and explicitly handles ranges that wrap across the 0 angle (min > max).

Bezier work uses fixed-order n=24 Legendre-Gauss quadrature: curveLength/curveLengthAtParameter integrate the tangent magnitude over the 24 precomputed abscissae/weights in constants.ts (LegendreGaussN24TValues / LegendreGaussN24CValues). The two arrays are paired by index and must stay length-24 and aligned.

curveIntersectLineSegment returns at most ONE intersection point, not all of them: it tries three hard-coded Newton-Raphson initial guesses (0.5/0.2/0.8) in order and returns the first that converges inside [0,1]x[0,1]; an empty array means no guess converged, which is not the same as 'no geometric intersection exists'.

curvePointAtLength/curveLengthAtParameter are numerical approximations gated by parameter clamps (percent<=0 returns endpoint t=0, >=1 returns t=1) and a 20-iteration binary search with tolerance = totalLength*0.0001 — results near the ends are exact endpoints, interior results are approximate.

polygon()/polygonFromPoints() auto-CLOSE the polygon: polygonClose appends a copy of the first point if the last point isn't already equal to it (closure tested via pointsEqual, i.e. within PRECISION). So a constructed Polygon always satisfies first==last; callers passing an already-closed ring won't get a duplicate.

The only outbound cross-package dependency is range.ts importing toBrandedType from '@excalidraw/common' (pinned to 0.18.0 in package.json). Every other src module depends only on sibling files inside this package — math is otherwise dependency-free.

Internal module dependency direction is types <- vector <- point <- {segment, ellipse, curve, polygon, rectangle}, with segment also depending on line. point.ts imports angle/utils/vector and is the most-imported sibling; vector.ts is a leaf (imports only types).

Two coexisting point-in-polygon algorithms with DIFFERENT edge semantics: polygonIncludesPoint uses an even-odd ray cast over a closed ring (i from 0, j = i-1 wrap), while polygonIncludesPointNonZero uses the nonzero winding rule over j=(i+1)%length and operates on raw [number,number] (not the branded Polygon). They can disagree on self-intersecting polygons — pick deliberately.

pointRotateRads short-circuits on a FALSY angle: 'if (!angle) return point' returns the SAME reference (not a copy) when angle is 0. Because angle is 0-checked via falsiness, a 0 rotation is a no-op identity return; non-zero angles return a fresh point.

pointTranslate carries a loud WARNING comment: it is NOT for translating Excalidraw element points because it does not account for element rotation; callers are told to use an element-aware translate instead. This is a deliberate boundary between the pure-math layer and element-aware higher layers.

Distance has paired squared variants intended for comparison-only fast paths: pointDistanceSq and vectorMagnitudeSq skip the sqrt and are documented 'use this if you only compare distances'. Mixing a squared value into an absolute-distance comparison is a latent unit error.

Three distinct conceptual primitives are encoded as the SAME 2-tuple-of-points shape and distinguished only by brand: Line

([p,q], infinite line), LineSegment

([a,b], bounded), and Rectangle

([topLeft,bottomRight], two opposite corners). They are structurally indistinguishable at runtime — the brand is the only thing preventing passing one where another is expected.

Ellipse is an axis-aligned, NON-rotated model at the type level: Ellipse

stores only {center, halfWidth, halfHeight} with no angle field, even though ellipse()'s JSDoc describes an 'angle' parameter. All ellipse math (ellipseIncludesPoint, ellipseDistanceFromPoint, intersections) assumes axis alignment; the JSDoc angle is stale relative to the type/signature.

TypeScript with strict branded (phantom-type) discipline is the deliberate technology choice for compile-time geometric type safety (coordinate-space and unit confusion are caught by the type checker via _brand intersections), at the cost of always needing factory constructors.

vectorFromPoint has a guard mode: when called with a positive 'threshold', vectors whose squared magnitude is below threshold^2 are replaced by a default unit vector [0,1] rather than returned as-is — a built-in degenerate/near-zero-vector fallback that callers may not expect when passing a threshold.

candidate_issues.claim: Brand collision: InclusiveRange is branded '_brand: "excalimath_degree"' (the SAME brand string as Degrees), not a range-specific brand. Copy-paste artifact — it weakens nominal typing because an InclusiveRange and a Degrees value become brand-indistinguishable, partially defeating the branding scheme this package relies on.

rangesOverlap has a logically dead branch: after 'if (a0 <= b0) return ...', the subsequent 'if (a0 >= b0)' can only be reached when a0 > b0, so its '>=' equality case is already handled by the first branch and the trailing 'return false' is unreachable for numeric inputs. The function is correct but contains dead code that can mislead a maintainer.

curveClosestPoint can return/propagate an undefined parameter: localMinimum declares 'k' uninitialized and only assigns it inside the while loop; if 'n - m > e' is false on entry the loop body never runs and 'k' (undefined) is returned, then passed as t to bezierEquation — producing NaN coordinates rather than a clean null. The '!solution' guard does not catch undefined-from-no-iteration.

segmentsIntersectAt returns null when u === 0 BEFORE computing the intersection point, dropping a valid intersection that lands exactly at segment b's start endpoint. Combined with the asymmetric bounds 't >= 0 && t < 1 && u >= 0 && u < 1' (inclusive low / exclusive high), endpoint-coincident intersections are treated inconsistently between the two segments.

Magic-tolerance coupling with no single source of truth: curve.ts embeds several unrelated literal tolerances (solver 1e-3, calculate 1e-2 / iterLimit 4, Jacobian det epsilon 1e-12, curveClosestPoint 1e-3, curvePointAtLength totalLength*0.0001), none tied to the package-wide PRECISION (1e-4). A reader cannot tell which tolerance governs a given closeness check, and changing precision policy requires editing many disconnected sites.

Two same-purpose point-in-polygon predicates with divergent semantics coexist (polygonIncludesPoint = even-odd ray cast; polygonIncludesPointNonZero = nonzero winding) and operate on different types (branded Polygon vs raw [number,number][]). Choosing the wrong one for self-intersecting / hole-containing polygons silently flips membership results.

extractions.role: A small, browser-targeted public utility package (@excalidraw/utils) that re-exports a stable export-to-image/clipboard API and a pure geometry/collision toolkit, both built as thin wrappers over the internal excalidraw, element, and math packages.

facts.claim: utils is the public npm package @excalidraw/utils (v0.1.2), an ES-module ('type':'module') published as a UMD/global ExcalidrawUtils for browser use; its purpose is to expose a curated subset of internal functionality (export + geometry) as a stable external API, not to hold core app logic.

The package public surface is the barrel index.ts which re-exports everything from export.ts plus two element-package functions (elementsOverlappingBBox, getCommonBounds); shape.ts geometry helpers are NOT re-exported through the barrel, so the geometry toolkit is an internal/secondary surface reached via subpath, not the main published API.

export.ts is a thin orchestration layer, not an implementation: every export entry point (exportToCanvas/exportToBlob/exportToSvg/exportToClipboard) delegates to functions imported from @excalidraw/excalidraw (scene/export, data/restore, data/json, data/image, clipboard, appState) — utils contributes only argument shaping and option defaults.

Inbound element/appState data is ALWAYS normalized through restoreElements (with deleteInvisibleElements:true) and restoreAppState before any export; this is a load-bearing precondition repeated identically in both exportToCanvas and exportToSvg, so exporters assume restored (not raw) input.

exportToBlob carries a non-obvious mimeType-coercion rule: the literal 'image/jpg' is silently rewritten to MIME_TYPES.jpg (the correct 'image/jpeg'), so callers passing the common 'image/jpg' typo are tolerated rather than rejected.

exportToBlob force-overrides appState.exportBackground to true for JPEG output (JPEG has no transparency) and only emits a console.warn — a silent behavioral coupling where the requested appState is mutated for correctness.

PNG-with-embedded-scene export deliberately serializes opts.elements (the ORIGINAL, uncloned elements) rather than the restoredElements actually rendered, with an inline NOTE that this keeps element ids stable across the Scene hack — a subtle ordering/identity invariant a refactor could easily break.

exportToClipboard dispatches on opts.type ('svg'|'png'|'json') and throws 'Invalid export type' on any other value — the type string, not the mimeType, is the routing key, and the three branches use three different clipboard primitives.

shape.ts intentionally defines a roughjs/element-INDEPENDENT pure-geometry layer ('pure shapes so we can provide collision and other geometric methods not depending on the specifics of roughjs or elements in Excalidraw') — a deliberate boundary decoupling geometry from element/render concerns, with conversion adapters living at the edge of this file.

shape.ts geometry is generic over a Point type parameter constrained to GlobalPoint | LocalPoint and an Ellipse models the rotated ellipse as {center, angle, halfWidth, halfHeight} (half-extents, deliberately NOT semi-major/minor) — the load-bearing data shape every ellipse function (pointInEllipse, ellipseAxes, distanceToEllipse) consumes.

The geometry layer depends entirely on @excalidraw/math for its point/vector/polygon primitives (pointFrom, pointRotateRads, vectorAdd, segmentsIntersectAt, polygon, curve, PRECISION, etc.) and on @excalidraw/element for getElementAbsoluteCoords/types — utils does not implement its own linear algebra.

distanceToEllipse computes nearest-point distance with a FIXED 3-iteration Newton-style refinement seeded at tx=ty=0.707 (cos/sin 45 degrees) — an approximation with a hardcoded iteration budget, so pointOnEllipse/threshold tests are approximate, not exact, by design.

getClosedCurveShape extracts polygon vertices from the roughjs Drawable by walking ops and toggling an 'odd' flag (taking only odd move-segments), because roughjs sketchy rendering produces doubled/overlapping stroke paths — a non-obvious workaround for the sketch backend's duplicated geometry.

perfect-freehand (package.json dependency) maps to the freedraw geometry: getFreedrawShape converts element.points to a polyline or, when isClosed, to a polygon — the only shape builder gated on a closed/open flag.

Test coverage is asymmetric and thin: the package ships 3 test files (export.test.ts, geometry.test.ts, utils.unmocked.test.ts) for 4 source files; the geometry test exercises only a subset of shape.ts's ~20 exported functions, so much of the ellipse/curve math is unverified by the package's own suite.

test-utils.ts is not production code: it registers a custom Jest matcher (toCloselyEqualPoints) for approximate point-array comparison, reflecting that geometry assertions are tolerance-based (default precision 2 -> ~0.01) rather than exact-equality.

The package targets browsers only and assumes DOM globals: exportToCanvas calls document.createElement('canvas') directly with no environment guard, so these utilities cannot run in a bare Node context — a hard runtime boundary.

Runtime image/clipboard dependencies are declared in package.json: pako (compression), png-chunk-text + png-chunks-encode/extract (PNG metadata embedding), @braintree/sanitize-url, browser-fs-access, roughjs, points-on-curve, perfect-freehand — the export+geometry feature set is built on these third-party libs.

candidate_issues.claim: getCurvePathOps guards only against a null/undefined Drawable (line 199), then if no 'path' set is found falls through to 'return shape.sets[0].ops' — an unguarded index into shape.sets[0]. A Drawable with a non-null but empty sets array yields undefined.ops and throws a TypeError, defeating the stated 'Temporary fix for extremely large elements' intent (which only handles the falsy-shape case). The empty-sets case is unhandled.

candidate_issues.family: IF-4/IF-10 (likely copy-paste defect: duplicated branch where a negation was intended)
candidate_issues.claim: ellipseExtremes returns 4 vectors; the yMax pair (lines 539-540) is correctly a +/- mirror (vector then vectorScale(...,-1)), but the xMax pair (lines 541-542) is two IDENTICAL entries 'vectorAdd(vector(xMax, yAtXMax), centerVector)' with no negation. By symmetry with the yMax pair, line 542 appears to have been intended as vectorScale(...,-1), so one extreme point is duplicated and the opposite x-extreme is never emitted.

extractions.role: Maintains a stable, collaboration-safe z-order for scene elements by keeping each element's string "fractional index" in sync with the array order, detecting/repairing invalid indices, and re-sorting elements by index during reconciliation and restore.

facts.claim: Two distinct layers: packages/fractional-indexing is a vendored, dependency-free base-62 fractional-indexing algorithm (generateKeyBetween/generateNKeysBetween/validateOrderKey), and packages/element/src/fractionalIndex.ts is the Excalidraw-domain layer that applies it to ExcalidrawElement arrays (validate/order/sync).

The base algorithm is vendored from the npm 'fractional-indexing' package under CC0, itself based on David Greenspan's 'Implementing Fractional Indexing' — a deliberate copy, not a runtime dependency, so it can be modified/audited in-tree.

Indices are base-62 strings ('0-9A-Za-z', digits in strict ascending char-code order) compared LEXICOGRAPHICALLY; a new key between two siblings is the string midpoint, so the order is a total order over strings and insertion never requires renumbering neighbors.

FractionalIndex is a branded string type (string & { _brand: 'franctionalIndex' }) — a nominal type to prevent passing arbitrary strings as indices; note the brand literal is misspelled ('franctionalIndex'), which is load-bearing because branded-type compatibility is by exact literal.

Core dual-structure invariant: the element array is the source-of-truth ordering CACHE and the fractional index encoded on each element must always be kept in sync with that array order; elements with invalid indices are detected and re-synced WITHOUT altering already-valid indices.

Validity of an element's index is positional, not absolute: isValidFractionalIndex requires predecessor < index < successor (lexicographically) against its array neighbors, plus a format check via validateOrderKey; a null/empty index is always invalid.

Tie-break rule for ordering: when two elements have IDENTICAL fractional indices, ties are broken by element id (a.id < b.id); when one element lacks an index, the array order is defensively preserved (sort comparator returns 1).

syncInvalidIndices MUTATES the passed elements in place (via mutateElement), whereas syncInvalidIndicesImmutable produces NEW element instances (via newElementWith) into a fresh map — two value-semantics variants for the same repair, the immutable one for store/delta paths.

Documented WARN: sync from the array can only GUESS which elements moved, so in edge cases syncInvalidIndices may rewrite the index of elements that were not actually moved — an accepted correctness/precision trade-off.

syncMovedIndices is the precise/optimistic path with a validate-before-commit guard: it generates candidate indices, runs validateFractionalIndices(shouldThrow:true) on the candidates BEFORE any mutation, splits mutation so it never leaves a half-updated inconsistent state, and on ANY throw falls back to the broad syncInvalidIndices.

Bound-text invariant: a bound text element's index must be strictly GREATER than its container's index (text.index > container.index), but this is validated only when includeBoundTextValidation is opt-in and is deliberately NOT auto-fixed by the sync functions.

validateOrderKey rejects the reserved key 'A' + 26 zeros (A0000...0) as invalid and rejects any key whose fractional part ends in a zero digit ('no trailing zeros') — these reserved/forbidden forms are why a syntactically base-62 string can still be an invalid index.

Repair works on CONTIGUOUS GROUPS bounded by valid lower/upper neighbors: getInvalidIndicesGroups/getMovedIndicesGroups emit groups whose first and last entries are the bounding indices, then generateIndices calls generateNKeysBetween(lowerBound.index, upperBound.index, n) to fill the interior — so a run of invalid elements is renumbered between the nearest valid anchors in one batch.
orderByFractionalIndex sorts IN PLACE via Array.prototype.sort (mutates the input array), not returning a new array — a value-semantics gotcha for callers expecting immutability.

Public surface (re-exported from the @excalidraw/element barrel): validateFractionalIndices, orderByFractionalIndex, syncMovedIndices, syncInvalidIndices, syncInvalidIndicesImmutable, and the InvalidFractionalIndexError class (code 'ELEMENT_HAS_INVALID_INDEX').

Reconciliation pipeline is the primary consumer: reconcile.ts syncs invalid indices on local+remote, validates them (shouldThrow with reconciliationContext for diagnostics), then orderByFractionalIndex on the reconciled set and re-syncs — i.e. fractional indices are the cross-client ordering authority for multiplayer.

restore.ts calls syncInvalidIndices when loading scenes — fractional indices are repaired on every restore for backwards compatibility with old scenes/libraries that predate the index field (index may be null for not-yet-assigned elements).

Scene.replaceAllElements uses syncInvalidIndices and Scene.insertElementsAtIndex uses syncMovedIndices, with validateFractionalIndices guarding scene mutations — the Scene is the runtime owner that keeps the live element array index-consistent.

The domain layer depends on mutateElement/newElementWith (./mutateElement), getBoundTextElement (./textElement), hasBoundTextElement (./typeChecks), arrayToMap (@excalidraw/common), and validateOrderKey/generateNKeysBetween (@excalidraw/fractional-indexing) — a one-directional dependency on the vendored algorithm package.

isOrderedElement classifies an element as 'ordered' purely by the PRESENCE of an index (element.index truthy), explicitly NOT by validity — a deliberately weak predicate meaning 'was ordered at some point', used to decide whether to apply index-based comparison vs. preserve array order.

candidate_issues.claim: syncMovedIndices wraps the entire optimistic path in a try/catch whose catch (e) silently discards the error 'e' and falls back to syncInvalidIndices with no logging — a validation failure during a move is swallowed with no diagnostic, masking systematic index-generation bugs. (The deeper syncInvalidIndices path then runs with ignoreLogs unset, so it is not fully silent, but the moved-path error itself is dropped.)

stringifyElement uses the non-null assertion on text.index!/container.index! while the comparison logic and isValidFractionalIndex elsewhere treat index as possibly null/empty; the bound-text check (text.index! <= container.index!) coerces possibly-null indices, which is an unsound spot if a bound element has a null index.

A pre-generated Graphify output graph.json sits inside the packages tree (packages/graphify-out/graph.json) and references fractionalIndex; per the project's own gotcha note this stray artifact can mis-tier the repo and pollute structural analysis — it should not be committed under packages/. (GATED — recorded, not a code defect in this component.)

extractions.role: The publishable React editor component library: it owns the public npm API barrel and the App god-component that wires together the in-memory Scene, the undo/redo History, the durable Store, an isolated Jotai state store, and the import/export/collaboration data layer.

facts.claim: This component is the npm package @excalidraw/excalidraw v0.18.0, an MIT-licensed React component library (its prod entry is dist/prod/index.js, types from excalidraw/index.d.ts); react/react-dom are peerDependencies (17/18/19), so the host app supplies React.

index.tsx is the public API surface: it defines the memoized <Excalidraw> component and is a large re-export barrel that re-exports both local modules (i18n, data/restore, data/json, data/blob, data/library, charts) AND symbols from sibling packages @excalidraw/element, @excalidraw/common, @excalidraw/utils — so the package's public API is an aggregation across the monorepo, not just its own code.

components/App.tsx is the central god-component (~13,054 lines) — by far the largest file; it is the runtime heart that constructs and owns Scene, Store, History, Renderer, ActionManager and Fonts and mediates nearly all editor behavior.

App constructs the core subsystems in a fixed order in its constructor: actionManager -> scene (new Scene()) -> canvas/renderer (new Renderer(scene)) -> store (new Store(this)) -> history (new History(store)) -> fonts (new Fonts(scene)); History depends on Store and Renderer/Fonts depend on Scene, so this construction order is load-bearing.

Editor state changes flow through the Store as the single source of increments, and History only subscribes to the Store: store.onDurableIncrementEmitter.on((increment) => this.history.record(increment.delta)). History is therefore a downstream listener of Store, never the driver — the boundary rule is Store -> History (one direction).

Undo/redo capture is governed by a CaptureUpdateAction tri-state passed to store on every scene mutation: IMMEDIATELY (immediately undoable, most local edits), NEVER (remote updates / scene initialization, never on undo stack), EVENTUALLY (deferred capture; the documented @default). Mis-tagging an update (e.g. a remote/collab update as IMMEDIATELY) would corrupt the undo stack — this is the key cross-cutting invariant for state changes.

History.record explicitly refuses to re-record entries that are already history-originated: it returns early if the delta isEmpty() OR is an instanceof HistoryDelta, preventing an undo/redo replay from being captured as a new undoable action (a re-entrancy guard).

History deliberately does NOT reset the redo stack on appState-only changes: it only clears redoStack when the recorded delta contains non-empty ELEMENT changes (if (!historyDelta.elements.isEmpty())), because a bare click/unselect (an appState change) must not wipe redo history. Subtle, non-obvious ordering/lifecycle rule.

When applying a history delta, the version/versionNonce properties are explicitly EXCLUDED from being restored (excludedProperties: new Set(['version','versionNonce'])) so that every undo/redo yields a NEW version — undo/redo is treated as a new user action for collaboration/sync purposes, not a literal time-travel restore.

Element versioning invariant: every element must have version > 0 so getSceneVersion() detects newly added elements — restore coerces version to (element.version || 1) and versionNonce to (?? 0). Code comment states the rationale directly.

Collaboration conflict resolution is deterministic and version-based: reconcileElements/shouldDiscardRemoteElement keep the local element when local is being actively edited (editingTextElement/resizingElement/newElement id match), OR local.version > remote.version, OR (equal versions) local.versionNonce <= remote.versionNonce — the equal-version tie is broken by the LOWEST versionNonce so all peers converge identically.

appState.ts defines APP_STATE_STORAGE_CONF — a per-field matrix declaring, for every AppState key, whether it is persisted to {browser, export, server}. This is the single source of truth for what AppState gets stripped per storage target; serializeAsJSON selects cleanAppStateForExport vs clearAppStateForDatabase based on it. Adding a new AppState field without an entry here silently fails to persist (the loop skips keys with no propConfig).

The editor uses an ISOLATED Jotai store (editorJotaiStore via jotai-scope createIsolation), not the global Jotai store — editor-jotai.ts wraps jotai with createIsolation() and exports a scoped Provider + a dedicated createStore(), so multiple Excalidraw instances and the host app's own Jotai atoms do not collide. index.tsx wraps App in <EditorJotaiProvider store={editorJotaiStore}>.

The host app reaches the running editor through React-context-bridged imperative API: ExcalidrawAPIContext / ExcalidrawAPISetContext (defined in App) + the ExcalidrawAPIProvider in index.tsx let useExcalidrawAPI()/useAppStateValue() work OUTSIDE the <Excalidraw> tree. This context-bridge is the supported boundary for external control of the editor.

Persistence to browser localStorage is centralized in the EditorLocalStorage class with keys typed to EDITOR_LS_KEYS, and every get/set/has/delete is wrapped in try/catch that console.warns and returns a safe fallback (false/null) — localStorage failures (quota, privacy mode) are swallowed, never thrown, so the editor degrades rather than crashing.

This component depends on sibling monorepo packages @excalidraw/element (Scene/Store/StoreDelta/CaptureUpdateAction/element mutation), @excalidraw/common (constants, Emitter, helpers), @excalidraw/math, and @excalidraw/utils — e.g. history.ts imports CaptureUpdateAction/StoreChange/StoreDelta and the Store/StoreSnapshot types from @excalidraw/element; the Store and Scene CLASSES live in @excalidraw/element, not in this package.

Component teardown (destroy) re-creates a fresh Scene/Renderer/Fonts and clears the store emitters in a fixed ordered sequence (renderer.destroy -> scene.destroy -> new Scene -> new Fonts -> new Renderer -> clear files/imageCache -> disconnect observers -> store.*Emitter.clear) — a defined lifecycle teardown order that must release the old graph before rebinding.

index-node.ts is a separate Node/SSR-oriented entry that bypasses the React component entirely and calls exportToCanvas directly with node-canvas (require('canvas'), registerFont) — distinct from the browser index.tsx entry, demonstrating the export pipeline is usable headless.

candidate_issues.claim: index.tsx (the public barrel) imports App from ./components/App, while components/App.tsx imports many sibling modules of the package; the public surface and the god-component are tightly coupled in a way that risks an import cycle between index.tsx <-> components/App.tsx <-> the re-exported local modules (data/*, charts, components/*). TypeScript/esbuild permit module cycles (no compile-time ban like Go/Rust), so this is structurally possible but I did NOT confirm an actual back-edge cycle from App.tsx back to index.tsx within this read — GATED, candidate only.

candidate_issues.family: IF-3a (magic-string / shared-key coupling without a shared constant)
candidate_issues.claim: appState.ts maintains TWO parallel structures keyed by the same AppState field names — getDefaultAppState() (the default values) and APP_STATE_STORAGE_CONF (the per-field persistence matrix). They must stay in lock-step by string key, but there is no single shared key list enforcing it; a new field added to one and not the other compiles (the storage loop simply skips keys with no propConfig, silently not persisting). Cross-structure coupling by field-name with no shared source of truth.

App's constructor assigns this.history = new History(this.store) twice — once at line 834 and again identically at line 842 with no intervening reassignment of this.store; the second assignment appears redundant (the first History instance is discarded). Grounded as a candidate; could be intentional defensive ordering but reads as dead/duplicate initialization.

score.notes:
DOC-COVERAGE 7/14 (50%). All 14 reference claims independently verified as CURRENT against the cloned repo at validation/_clones/excalidraw (anchors all accurate: root package.json yarn workspaces, esbuild in scripts/buildBase.js, vite in excalidraw-app, vitest.config.mts aliases, tsconfig strict:true, examples/with-nextjs + with-script-in-browser, all package names).

COVERED WELL — the published-package skeleton. Matched all 5 core component-exists claims for the published packages (@excalidraw/excalidraw RC-002, common RC-006, element RC-007, math RC-008, utils RC-009), the npm-publish technology-choice (RC-003: 'one publishable React component library' = @excalidraw/excalidraw), and TypeScript-throughout (RC-014, explicitly 'an ESM-only TypeScript monorepo' + corroborated by the branded-nominal-type/import-type erasure discussion). The understanding goes FAR DEEPER than the thin reference on these packages (24 key-invariant/boundary/coupling facts on undo-redo, versioning, binding, etc.), but those have no reference counterpart so they neither add nor subtract coverage.

MISSED — two whole layers the reference documents and the understanding (package-centric, blind to the doc) omitted: (1) the BUILD/TEST TOOLING technology-choices — Yarn workspaces (RC-001; understanding says 'monorepo' but never names the workspace tool — no near-miss credit on the tech-choice distinguisher), esbuild (RC-012), Vite (RC-013), and the vitest.config.mts path-alias boundary-rule (RC-011); (2) the APPLICATION/EXAMPLES layer — excalidraw-app/ (RC-004, a private:true app, which is exactly why a package-only framing dropped it), its dependency edge excalidraw-app -> @excalidraw/excalidraw (RC-005), and examples/ (RC-010). Per-kind: component-exists 5/7, technology-choice 2/4, dependency-edge 0/1, boundary-rule 0/1.

DOC-FAITHFULNESS 7/7 (100%). Of the understanding facts overlapping a reference topic (the TS monorepo framing, the publishable @excalidraw/excalidraw library, and the 5 packages' existence/roles), every one is correct and verified against code: ESM-only confirmed (all packages "type":"module"), element runtime deps = common+math (+vendored fractional-indexing the understanding correctly splits out), publishable React component library confirmed. Nothing in the understanding contradicts the reference or the code.

deepinit_wrong_high = 0 — HARD GATE PASSES. No HIGH-certainty fact is refuted by the reference or code. The understanding's omissions are pure coverage gaps (it stayed within the published-package boundary it could ground), never confident errors.

logs: PROXY baseline (committed excalidraw.json) was 11/14 = 78.6% — compare for the proxy-vs-real delta
